summary: I have a deep connection to Florida, especially Jacksonville where I settled in 1906. My musical career flourished here, and I was involved with the early days of radio broadcasting at WJAX and the Florida Times-Union station. Florida has been home to my family and my work, including my daughter's dancing school in West Palm Beach. It’s a place where I witnessed the growth of music and culture firsthand.

My mother was a small girl during the American Civil War, which we also call the war between the States. She was born in Athens, Georgia, in 1844, so she experienced the war years as a child. The memories and songs from that era, including plantation songs and spirituals, have influenced my musical work. I even recorded some of those old songs that she and others remembered from that time.

Athens, Georgia, is where my mother was born in 1844 and where I attended public schools and a private girls' school. It was also the home of my family’s early roots, including my grandfather who settled there after fleeing Germany. I cherish the education I received in Athens, especially under Nies Caine Oenowski, which helped shape my musical talents. My family history is deeply tied to this city.

Heinrich von der Lieth was my maternal grandfather, a German who fled compulsory military training in Germany and settled in Charleston, South Carolina before moving to Athens, Georgia. He was the son of a tailor to the Emperor of Germany and had a proud heritage of embroidering royal crests. His story and background have always been a part of my family history and identity.

My grandfather’s father was tailor to the Emperor of Germany, not Kaiser Wilhelm but his father. He had the important duty of embroidering the royal crest and coat-of-arms on the Emperor’s clothing and household linens. This connection to the German imperial household is a proud part of my family’s heritage and history.

Hanover, Germany, is where my grandfather was originally from before he fled to America. It was his native city and the place tied to our family’s German roots. His departure from Hanover was due to his disapproval of compulsory military training, which led him to start a new life in the United States.

Josiah Bartlett was one of my father’s famous ancestors and a signer of the Declaration of Independence. I have an old book with a reproduction of the Declaration where his name is prominently displayed. This connection to American history is something I have always treasured in my family lineage.

My father’s ancestors came to America on the famous Mayflower, which is a significant part of my heritage. This connection to early American history has always been a point of pride in my family. It ties us to the founding stories of the nation and its earliest settlers.

I possess a very old book containing a reproduction of the Declaration of Independence, where my ancestor Josiah Bartlett’s name is prominently written. This document symbolizes a proud part of my family’s history and America’s founding. It connects me personally to the nation’s early struggles for freedom and independence.

Grant Park in Atlanta was given to the city by Leonard Grant, my father’s paternal uncle. It has become a beautiful landmark in Atlanta, and I feel a connection to it through my family. Knowing that my family contributed something lasting to the city is a source of pride for me.

Atlanta is where Grant Park, a landmark given by my family, is located. One of my sisters lives near Atlanta, and the city figures into my family’s history and present. It’s a place tied to both our heritage and ongoing family life.

Nies Caine Oenowski was the Russian lady who ran the private girls’ school in Athens that both my mother and I attended. She was a woman of education, culture, and refinement and played a key role in my early education, especially in music. She recognized my talent and helped secure a scholarship for me at the Chicago Musical College.

Charles Horty was connected to my school through marriage; Miss Oenowski’s niece married him. He was famous for his experiments in the turpentine industry and for discovering a process to convert scrub slash pine into commercially used paper. His work was notable in the pulp paper industry, and I remember hearing about his contributions during my schooling.

I was awarded a year’s scholarship to the Chicago Musical College thanks to Miss Oenowski’s recommendation. My training there was wonderful and exposed me to many famous musicians and theatrical personalities. Although I was more involved with theatrical elements in Chicago, the experience greatly enriched my musical skills and career.

After Chicago, I went to Boston to study at the Paelton Pianoforte School, where I graduated after rigorous training. Boston was culturally rich, and I met many notable people including Maud Elliott and heard great concerts like those by Yaw. The city’s musical environment helped polish my skills and gave me valuable exposure to professional recitals.

The Paelton Pianoforte School in Boston was where I studied intensively, practicing six hours a day and graduating with high marks. The school emphasized attending professional recitals and performing polished concerts, which greatly enhanced my poise and technique. It was a crucial step in my development as a pianist.

I knew Josef Hofmann personally during my studies, and his kindly interest and advice influenced me greatly. Meeting such a renowned musician was inspiring and helped shape my approach to piano playing. His mentorship was a memorable part of my musical education.

While in Chicago, I met Joseph Jefferson, a famous interpreter and theatrical figure. Though my focus was music, I was drawn into the theatrical world there and encountered many notable personalities like him. These experiences enriched my artistic perspective beyond just music.

Richard Mansfield was another theatrical personality I met in Chicago. His presence and work in the theater world influenced the environment I was part of, blending music with drama. Meeting him was part of my broader exposure to the arts during my studies.

Louis Morrison was renowned for his portrayal of Mephistopheles in Faust, and I met him during my time in Chicago. His performances were well known, and his artistry added to the rich theatrical culture I experienced. This connection helped me appreciate the dramatic side of musical performance.

Faust was a significant work in the theatrical circles I encountered, especially noted for Louis Morrison’s portrayal of Mephistopheles. The play’s music and drama were part of the cultural experiences that influenced my artistic development. It was a classic that many of the performers I met were associated with.

Maud Elliott was a beautiful and well-known star I met while studying in Boston. Her presence in the cultural scene was impressive and added to the rich artistic environment I was part of. Meeting her was one of the memorable experiences of my time there.

I was involved with WJAX, the municipal broadcasting station in Jacksonville, especially when it opened with long programs after Thanksgiving Day in 1926. I served as accompanist for several numbers and played for actors like Henry Cornely and Professor LePaige. Being part of WJAX’s early days was thrilling and expanded my musical reach through radio.

The Florida Times-Union building in Jacksonville housed the first little radio station where I eagerly assisted in broadcasts. This early involvement with radio music distribution was exciting and marked a new chapter in my career. The station was located behind the Times-Union on Trent Avenue street.

Thanksgiving Day in 1926 was memorable because WJAX began its long broadcasting programs then, lasting until three o’clock the next morning. I was an accompanist during these programs, which marked a significant moment in Jacksonville’s musical and radio history. It was a festive and important occasion for local music.

Henry Cornely was one of the performers I accompanied on WJAX radio in Jacksonville. Playing for him and his associates was part of my work during the early days of radio broadcasting. It was rewarding to support such talented artists in live broadcasts.

I have been associated with the Federal Music Project in Florida since its establishment in 1936. It has been a vital program for both older musicians like myself and younger talent needing coordinated training. I played as accompanist in many concerts and believe the project is a great humanitarian effort worth continuing.

Alongside the Federal Music Project, I worked with the Federal Theatre Project in Florida, accompanying concerts and public entertainments. This involvement helped sustain musicians and performers during difficult times. The project was an important cultural support system that I valued highly.

I admire President Franklin D. Roosevelt and believe his administration represents the greatest humanitarian endeavor in history. His support for programs like the Federal Music Project has been invaluable to musicians and artists. I am hopeful he will accept a third term because I fully support his leadership and vision.
